text: The U.S.-German Joint Statement did not make Nord Stream 2 completion contingent on Ukraine’s consent, but rather outlined measures to mitigate its impact on Ukraine.
Critics in Ukraine and Poland described the deal as insufficient and questioned the binding nature of its security and economic commitments.
The agreement relies heavily on the prospect of new sanctions if Russia uses energy as a weapon, with Germany pledging action at national and EU levels.
Germany committed to safeguard and increase reverse gas flow capacity to Ukraine, though analysts noted this pledge lacked enforceable mechanisms.
A shared objective of extending the Ukraine-Russia gas transit agreement beyond 2024 was confirmed, with Germany appointing a special envoy to facilitate up to a 10-year extension.

The U.S.-Germany Climate and Energy Partnership established a Green Fund for Ukraine, with initial German contributions of at least $175 million aiming to mobilize $1 billion for decarbonization efforts.
The article accurately summarizes these commitments but does not include direct quotes from the agreement text, which may limit evaluation of its alleged vagueness and non-binding nature.
Maria Shagina is a postdoctoral fellow at the University of Zurich and a member of the Geneva International Sanctions Network, lending expert perspective to the analysis.
